But as most experts soon find out, no matter how much personal development you achieve, there will always be a point where you can no longer progress. The only way to continue to grow is by helping others become like you. Yes, true growth and fulfillment comes from your contribution to others. Your message has the ability to change someone's life. The impact that the right message can have on someone at the right time in their life is immeasurable. You can help to save marriages, repair families, change someone's health, grow a company or more. But only if you know how to get in the hands of people whose lives you have been called to change. Expert secrets will help you find the voice and give you the confidence to become a leader. Expert secrets will show you how to build a mass movement of people whose lives you can affect. Expert secrets will teach you how to make this calling a career where people will pay you for your advice. And nowadays though, it's like, really good copy is good to get cold people in. But if you're a jerk or your movement sucks or you don't have those things, then everything falls apart. You can sell someone once, but you can't keep selling them unless you build something, unless you do this foundational stuff we're talking about today. You do that, then you can become really bad at selling and keep making money because you've built this tribe of people who love you, they trust you, and it won't buy anything you basically recommend. And it makes it so you... It's kind of this weird thing where you don't have to have as good of skills, but because people will buy because of the personality, but at the same time, by doing it, the way we'll talk about it, your skills get better and better, and by having both of those things together, it's insane what can happen. And so, I mean, I'm just looking at our company. I spent, man, the first eight or nine years of our business stuck at like, probably three or $4 million a year. Let's do more of that. So that's kind of the goal. All right, now a couple of things just to preface this. So who is this for? Basically there's two types of businesses that this works for. Number one is the one that a lot of you guys are doing your information businesses. So you're selling courses, coaching, info products, books, seminars, all that kind of stuff. So this expert secrets process obviously works for you guys. The second side of this, and this has been a fun one for me, is using this expert stuff, this business to take other businesses and to explode them. And click funnels right now. I think Dave has the opportunity always to meet with all the VCs. We have, is it three or four different VCs? Seven. Oh. Seven venture capitalist companies who are trying to like throw money at us. And it's like the most funny thing because they kept telling us like you guys are unicorn, like what you're doing makes no logical sense. Like you're growing and like insane numbers without any money, any cash, anything. How does this work? And so I'm like, well it's different because we're building a cult. Don't you understand? And they're like, no, I don't understand that. Like, okay, well, what's cool is like if you have this expert business like you as the personality and you tie it to something else. Like we've got me being the puppet doing my thing. But then you got click funnels as this business. It's like this shot of adrenaline, this fuel that will just dramatically grow those other businesses. I'm watching as Brandon K. L. Newn is now with the supplement company coming out. They're the expert, the supplement company is going to explode because of what they're doing. Any business that if you can wrap it with an expert in front of it can grow fast. One good example, Tony Robbins. He is not a financial expert, but he partnered with these dudes who had financial, it's over my head, the financial side of the stuff. And he wrote this book about how to make money. He goes and launches this book. He's on every single talk show. Weebel the funnel forum is selling books like crazy. And he sells all these books. He gives 100% of all the money back to charity and feeding families and all this kind of stuff. And people are like, man, Tony really loves the world. But then Tony's this attractive character bringing these leads in like crazy, ships him off to this company now who's doing all of the investing and that kind of stuff. And I talked to him like six months after the book launched. My numbers may be wrong, but I think he told me in the first six months they raised $6 billion. And that was like a year and a half ago. As of right now, I don't know what it is, but it's insane. There's no other company that can grow like that because most of them it's just like, it's a company, it's a static boring company. But you wrap an expert in front of it, it's like fuel and it makes things blow up. And you can look at most businesses that have an expert in front of them and then there's business in the back. And that's I think the best and the fastest way to grow. In fact, when we're talking the VCs, I did a whole podcast on this, like how to confuse the venture capitalists, which was really funny. So we're sitting there and he's like, so what's your cost to acquire a customer? I'm like, well, right now it costs us about $120 to get somebody to join ClickFunnels. He's like, oh, that's really, really like in their world, that's a really good number. Like cool, so you just need more money to blow to get more people. And I was like, no, we actually turned those ads off. He's like, why'd you turn the heads off? I was like, well, because what we do instead is we build these funnels, people want my book or this or they buy things like that. And then those funnels are actually profitable. So we spend $15 to sell a book. We make $33 through the funnel. So we get a customer basically giving us whatever the difference, $13 bucks or $18, whatever that is. And then we introduce them to ClickFunnels. So basically they come to us, they give us cash and then we tell them about our product. So we actually get paid $18 for every person we introduce to ClickFunnels. And he's like, that doesn't make any sense. So I explain it three or four or five times. Dave is there with me. He's like, this doesn't make any sense. I'm like, well, that's the world we live in. So when you guys understand this, he said at the end of lunch, he said, if this is true, what you're talking about is actually possible. You guys could change every business across the world. I'm like, well, it is true and we are. So it's kind of cool. Now the other thing that's cool is when you look at this for a more traditional business, when you wrap this with an expert business, first thing is it simplifies the sell. Secondly, I'm just like, Tony Robbins is the man. I'm going to go give that dude some money. I don't even care who he is. I don't even care. Tony said it's good. I'm in. You don't have to go and spend six months trying to explain why our algorithm is better or why are all these things. They don't care. They trust you and so they're in. So it simplifies the sell. Next thing, price resistance literally disappears because you're no longer a commodity. How many of you guys, I probably shouldn't ask, how many of you guys feel like you're in a commodity business where everyone's selling something similar? The problem with the commodity business is that's the worst business to be in because everyone's pushing the price to the lowest thing possible. And when that's happening, that's the worst business in the world to be in, to be fighting for the lowest price. Dan Kennedy, I heard him once say, he said that you can't be the lowest price leader in your group. There's no strategic advantage of being the second lowest price leader. So you got to be the highest because there's a strategic advantage of being the highest. There's nothing in the middle there. It's like for us, it's like when you build that thing, you just keep raising the prices and price resistance is gone because they want you. They don't care about the thing you're selling. They want access to you and they will pay for that. And then the last thing is free unlimited customers. Like I said, the VC people understand, we're growing our company. It's not costing us any money to grow it. And that's the magic. So that's kind of the two sides of this whole business. Obviously, we're focusing on the expert side. It doesn't matter what kind of business you have. If you structure it right, it's the fuel for both of them. All right. So this right here is what we're going to be covering today, basically. These are the three things that you need to build a cult. Sure. And this was kind of a fun thing. So I've written the expert secrets book three times before we finished it and finally went to print. And a year and a half is a bit of a nightmare. But I think it turned out good, hopefully. But when I was in Kenya with my wife and we were at WorldT Trade and we were in these little tiny van things sitting on this road. And they're all dirt roads. We're bumping like crazy as we're going through this thing. And it's hard to hear. It's hard to talk. And my wife passed out and everyone else is bumping in. It's funny. I was like, I can't communicate with anybody because it's so long. We had a four hour drive in this thing. So I got out a couple of books I brought with me. I don't know if it's true believer by Eric Hoffer, which is like how to build a mass movement. And then there was the three or four other ones. I'm going through these books and I'm reading them. And I start looking at patterns. For me, I think what I'm really good at is I look at a bunch of stuff and I see these weird patterns. Like, whoa. Do you guys see that pattern? That's awesome. And so you see the pattern and then you can model it and you can use it again. So I'm reading all these books and the books were good but they're kind of all over the place. And as I'm doing that, I'm taking numbers and actually different things. And I realized that there were three. And every mass movement they talked about in all these books, there were three things that all of those had. And it didn't matter if you were looking at which end of the spectrum. If you looked at the worst of the worst, you look at someone like Hitler, he had these three things. And he looked at the best of the best, someone like Christ, he had these three things. And everyone in between. And it was so interesting looking at how the patterns were the same. And every mass movement in the history of beginning of time and we'll continue to go forth and have all these three things. Okay, number one is a charismatic leader. Number two is a cause of something that's happening in the future. And number three is a new opportunity. And so we're going to go in deep into all three of these because this is the foundation of it. When you have these three things, you can create a mass movement. When you don't have them, you can sell some products, you can do ones you choose and you'll have some stuff. But if you want to get people to follow you and have lasting change, all these kind of things, these are the three pieces. And it's been interesting as I've become aware of these things now, then it's, again, since you become aware of a model or a pattern, then you can go and you can tweak things based on that. And so since then, which has been now probably seven or eight months ago since we were there, we started looking at all these things and like, hey, let's try this. Let's try this. And something's worked really good, something's didn't work. We kept doing things on all three of these different things. And those changes have been insane. In fact, those are the podcasts. December alone, we did, like last year was a good year. We did it eight figures. But we did, in December alone of this year, we did half of what we did all of. Last year, which is, like, doesn't make any logical sense, but it's all these things, like, layering on top of each other throughout the whole year. And then it's like, just things keep growing and growing and growing. And so we're going to go deep into all of these and we're going to be workshopping things. But it's kind of the model and the pattern that if we understand it, then we can replicate it. And then it's the foundation for all the other stuff that we're trying to do. Okay. Next slide. All right. So, as I was thinking through this whole thing, I read an article, I've read this a couple of times. Anyone here read the article, A Thousand True Fans? Okay. So this is by Kevin Kelly. And the article is awesome. Tim Ferriss talked about it in his most recent book as well. It was just an article, or like a blog post, something he wrote called A Thousand True Fans. And this was kind of the key piece in there, he said, that a creator such as an artist, a politician, photographer, craftsperson, performer, animator, designer, video maker, author. In other words, anyone producing works of art, which is what we're all doing. Okay. It's a work of art. Needs to acquire only a thousand true fans to make a living. A true fan is defined as someone who will purchase anything and everything you produce. They will drive 200 miles to see you sing. They will buy your super hot deluxe, reissued high res box set of your stuff, even though they have the low res version. They have a Google alert set up for your name. They bookmark the eBay pages where you opt out of print and editions show up. They come to your openings. They have your signed copies. They will buy your t-shirts, the mug, the hat. They can't wait until you issue your next work. They are your true fans. And so that's what we're talking about. How do we build thousand true fans? And if you can do that, that's when this whole game becomes insanely easy. They'll buy from you over and over and over again, and that's when all these fun things happen and you start growing that. So for me, this has been our focus. It's like thousand true fans, thousand true fans. One thing we're doing right now is we're releasing a lot of free plus shipping offers just to get our customers and our fans buying consistently from us. There's something about repetition and I want people to buy from us, hopefully every day, but at least every week or two. And as we're doing that, we'll watch you. We put it out there and as we're watching how many people are buying and pretty consistently, just from the initial push, we get 2,000 or 3,000 people to buy every single thing we put just like, hey, this is live. Go buy it and then they go buy it. I'm like, cool. It's kind of a gauge where we can see who are true fans. Who's going to buy my stupid marketing in your car, MP3 player, even though they can get every single issue or episode for free on iTunes. They're all free, but who's going to buy it? Who are my true fans? You find out, boom, there's the people and they keep buying over and over and over again. It's like if you can create things that get people that way, that's the goal. Because you have that, like the article said, you're set for life. That's when you can say you're free or whatever you want to call it, financially free. Okay. All right. Okay. So the first section here we're going to talk about is the charismatic leader or the attractive character. How many of you guys right now, guys and gals, are the attractive character in your business? How many of you guys there are somebody else that's kind of like the face of it? Cool. So I got kind of a little bit of both. So this is the key. Every movement has this, has a charismatic leader, attractive character. There's not any of them that don't. Can you think of one? I can. Apple, Steve Jobs, Christianity, he's got Christ. There's every single thing, there's one. He's very charismatic. But that's the key essential thing. So it's like, okay, now we know that. Like what do we know about these people? What can we reverse engineer to figure out for ourselves? And one thing is, the biggest thing as I've been talking about is book and sharing more and more people. I get tons of resistance from people. Like, I'm not expert. I don't want to, you know, and like there's always that thing, right? And I understand that. Like the feeling of the vignette was like, I don't know if I'm good enough. I don't know if I'm smart enough. I don't know if like, like, I don't know about you guys, but I still feel that. Like I've been so nervous about this. I've put so much time and effort because like I'm scared to death to get up. Like you sent me jumping like a little kid back. So excited. I'm so nervous because like I want to produce for you guys. I don't want any of you guys to be like, man, that was a waste of three days, right? Like I have the same inadequacies. Like I'm nervous. Like, and everyone has that. But like, and this kind of goes back to talking about the beginning of the book. Like for all of us, like there's a time where we go through this phase and there's always different phases in life, right? But there's a phase where like you figured out your thing, your craft and you got excited. You started studying and you like, you geeked out and you got better and better and better at it. But there's a point where like it doesn't matter how much better you get. Like you can't, like there's a point you can't keep progressing, right? Like Tony Robbins, if you've been to his event, he talks about like, yeah, I read 700 books in like a year and he's like, now what? Like, I got like, I know everything, but now what? And you can't, like you, you physically, you stop your progression. You cannot move from that point until you help somebody else. The first time I ever saw this was that I ever understood it was my senior year. I was, I'd wrestled all through high school. I was a state champ senior year took second place in the country. And I was like about to go into college. And I was like, I thought I was like the coolest guy in the world, right? And my coach said, hey, you want to come coach at the wrestling camp this summer? I was like, that would be so cool. I think they paid like 10 bucks a day. I'm like, I'm going to be rich. I'm not about almost 100 bucks at the end of this camp. I was really excited. So we go to this camp and he's like, hey, teach these kids how to do the things you're good at. So I'm okay, so I'm teaching these kids and I show them like, this is the move. Okay, go do it. And they do it. I'm like, crap, none of them did it. I'm like, okay, no, do it like this. And they did it. And then like no one could do it. I'm like, dang it. I'm going to do it real quick and watch me. So I did it and I'm like, what am I doing? I was like, okay, my elbows here, my hips are here. I'm like, oh, that's why it works. And I find this pattern, right? It's like we're doing now. Like I find the pattern. I'm like, oh, so it only works if my knees here, my hips here and the pressure tight here. So I showed the kids, okay, you got it. And I showed them that. And all of a sudden they get it. And I was like, wow. And then I realized like that's the pattern. And then I'm like, that was happening before. Like I was, I'd done so many times that I was good at it, but I didn't know the reason. So in college, I was like, that summer was huge because I learned all the reasons why I was doing things. I saw the pattern and as I would go back and become better and better because I was like, man, if things aren't working, it's because I'm missing one of those three or four points. And I became a much better wrestler because of it. Same thing in this business. Like there was a time where I was just like, you see my, those who've done an office tour, like you see the library. It's ridiculous. And that's after we threw away an entire dumpster, like probably four or five feet above the dumpster full of courses when we moved here. So that's like what's left over that made it through the trash. And like I went through a phase where I was just geeking out on everything. Like I just consumed as much as I could. I was implementing, I was doing some stuff and having fun, but it was like, I couldn't keep progressing until I, like there's so much stuff I just wanted to share it. And then like as I started sharing it, that's when I progressed, right? Like I'm sure you guys as a good example, like you guys took all the stuff I'm sharing, you went and you've changed like how many people's lives have you guys affected now? And like that is growth for me. Like I can't do what Kailin and Brandon, I can't do that. I can't do any of you guys do, right? But the fact that I can now share this stuff and you go and you do it and you affect a million people, a million women lose all its way. Like, holy crap, like that, like that fills me up more than anything that I can do on my own, right? Like there's a point where the only way you can progress is by doing that and taking that leap of faith from like being a student to being an expert. And so it's scary and it's freaked, like it freaks me out, but we all have to be willing to take that leap of faith. And I got a quote for you guys from Garrett White for those who are need a little extra pushing and convincing. I actually quoted this in my book and publishers like, you can't say that. It's not proper English. I'm like, it doesn't matter. It's right. Okay, so listen, this is very important. So first off, understand no matter what you try in the beginning, it's going to suck because you suck. But you'll get better and you'll suck less as you keep doing this. And eventually you'll suck less so little that you'll actually be good. But just surrender to the fact that you're going to suck. All right, can you guys all surrender to the fact you're going to suck for a little bit? I'm talking and I wouldn't even dare look at the camera. That's how scared I was. And that was like four years ago. And it's funny because now people are like, well, you're so good in front of camera, you're so good on stage. I'm like, are you kidding me? Then I walked back off and next time I come out, okay, please let someone by. Please let someone by. Do my thing and one dude bought. I'm like, why'd you buy? He's like, could you say this? I'm like, okay, cool, I'll say more of that. And they kept doing it over and over. And I was on the road for three years doing the public speaking circuits, two or three times a month flying somewhere speaking over and over and over again to learn how to actually do it. The reason I'm going on video now is because I've done so many videos over and thousands of videos. And that's it. That's just consistent repetition over and over and over again. Okay, when I first, I always tell people like, when I first started my marketing car podcast, we set up the RSS feed wrong so we couldn't track stats. So I had no idea I'm people listening until like three weeks ago we finally got it fixed. So for three years I had no idea. And I'm so grateful I never knew because if I would have known like, wow, there's only two people listening, I would have stopped. Okay, now I'm 300 episodes in and we have this huge following. We finally got the RSS feed figured out like three weeks ago. We're already like 400,000 listens in like three weeks. I'm like, crap, people are listening. But they weren't at first. But 42 is like when I started actually like, you gave some good things. I'm like, cool, I don't know. I was just talking in my phone while I was driving because that's what I knew how to do. Okay, so don't be worried about it. It's just like it's consistently doing these things over and over and over again. We had Andrew O'Brien, our PR stuff was here last weekend at the certified partner event and then took him to lunch afterwards. And what was cool, this comes back to creating offers, but all these things, he's just like, yeah, he's like, I just go and I create something and I try to sell it. And nobody buys it and I create something else. So he showed me over the last like six weeks, he launched like five or six different programs. He's like, I launched that one nobody bought, so I just didn't do it. Then I launched this program, nobody bought, so I didn't do it. Then I launched this one and people went crazy, we sold a hundred of them. And I was like, what's the difference? He's like, I just kept making him do things and seeing what people bought. And then they bought that one, so then we went deep and made it how much thing and then I tried this one nobody bought and this one nobody bought. But the five things I put out there is like one thing people bought, so then went and did more of that. It's the same thing with like this, you being an expert, like doing Facebook lives or Snapchat, all these different things you're doing, you're putting out there. It's less about like making money right now. It's more about like finding your voice and getting comfortable in that, in that state and being good with yourself. And then as you're putting things out there, you're gonna find out what people actually resonate with. A good example is Anthony when he launched the Biohacking Seekers book. First off, it wasn't working very good. And so we got on a call, I was like, hey, you need to do a Facebook live a day every single day, tell forever. He's like, all right, man. And then I was like, do you remember I told you, I'm like, do all the weird stuff. I get the lights on your head, stuff up your nose, and that's gonna go like crazy viral. And he did and nobody cared. But then he did this one, it was like how to biohack your vegetables. And I was like, dude, that's like the stupid, like put the lights in your nose while you're doing that. And biohacking your vegetables got like three or four million views. And you don't know what people want. So you gotta put out a whole bunch of stuff out there. And he's got dozens of videos now that have gone crazy viral, and even more that have failed, but it's consistency. I think for now you're pretty dialed in, like you know what people react to and what they don't now. And that's the key is just consistently doing over and over and over again. And being okay, feeling stupid when they don't. Because that's the coolest thing that nobody knows. Like if you fail, yeah, nobody really knows, which is kind of nice. All people really know it's yourself. But if you don't tell anybody, then nobody knows. In fact, the very first event I ever did, I was at, I joined, Matt Fury had this mastermind program, so I joined it. And the very first call, he was on there, it was so funny. The guy was like, some guy called and said, I have a question. Like I'm in the weightlifting market and we lift, and I lift weights and, like how do you guys should grow my business? Matt's like, you know what you should do, man? You should put on an event at your house for $5,000. And people will come to you and lift weights in your garage with you. He's like, can people buy that? He's like, oh yeah, sweet man, all right, I'll do that. Next I get started. Hey, so I write articles for living and I submit them and like, what should I do? You guys think you gotta work, Matt? It's like, yeah. I was like, huh. And then we had three people in a row. He told the exact same advice. And so I'm sitting there like, I'm gonna, yeah, I'm gonna put on an event for $5,000 in my house and see people do it. So I like that night, send an email out to my list and I was like, there's this event in my house, it's $5,000, amazing. And the next day I wake up, look at my PayPal account, nobody bought. I'm like, oh, that sucks. Well, nobody knows. And then the next day, two orders came through. I was like, oh crap, it's made $10,000. I'm like, oh my gosh. That was insane for me. I was like, two people bought. And first I was all excited, like celebrating. And I was like, oh man, if we go to an event and only two people are there, I'm like an idiot. I was like, I'm not giving that money back either. So I called everyone I knew, like my friends, my family, that's all like Brent. I called Brent, we just became friends. I'm like, hey, I'm doing this event. Everyone paid $5,000. Can you come and not tell anybody about it? That'd be so nice. So he came, my friends came, every day I knew came and I was like, just don't tell those two that you didn't pay because it's gonna be really awkward. So I did my first event that way. And it was super ghetto too. We're in this hotel and we had the camera set up and there was windows behind it and the windows were shining, the camera guy was like, I can't see anything. So we got duct tape and we were duct taping up blankets from the hotel bedroom to cover and like, anyway, it was horrible. But it worked and people had a great experience and it got me to do my first event and then from there, anyway, everything kind of started. But anyway, it just has to do with just like trying it and just who knows what's gonna work but being okay with like, it's probably gonna fail because the first year you're gonna suck. But eventually you're gonna suck so little, it'll actually be good. That's the plan at least. Okay. So now we kind of talked through that stuff a little bit. Next I want to go into, in the dot-com Seekers book in chapter number one or Seeker number one. I talk about this concept and this is something that I wish when I got started, somebody would grab me and like held my head and like yell at me and told me this because I got out there and first thing I'm doing is I'm making stuff because it's like, oh, I'm gonna make a product. I made a product and then somebody bought and I'm like, oh, so I made another one and I kept doing this and eventually six years later, I woke up one day with a hundred employees, whole bunch of customers that I hated. Like, to be completely honest, they were horrible and I was just like miserable. I remember laying there in bed one day and I was like, I don't want to get up. I don't care about the people I'm serving. I have a hundred people. Seriously, I'm like 11 year old kid. Like, God, people's lives depend on me actually producing. That was scary. And I remember sitting there thinking and I told my wife, I was like, I wish I had a boss. She's like, why am I? So he could fire me and I could be done with this. I'm like, that's the wrong thing when you have a big business and people like, that's not the right thing. But I was just like guessing, right? And so I'm like, a little bit after that, we had this really cool period of time where we almost went bankrupt and everything almost fell apart and I had to fire like 80 people in a day and it was like the worst pain that I've ever experienced. It was hard. I had some of my best friends and family members walk out on me and it was a man, super humbling and horrible, hard experience. But it gave me a really cool chance to kind of reset and then be like, God, I want to do this? Like, that was horrible. What should I do? What do I want to be when I grow up and kind of start thinking through that again? And as I started thinking through that, I was like, okay, well, if I'm going to do this again, like first off, I don't want those customers because they're annoying. Like I want a different kind of customer. So I started thinking through like, who would I actually want to serve? Like what would my dream? And I remember like, as we were kind of doing this, we had launched other, like we had our supplement, we had other businesses that were kind of starting to happen during that time. And it was kind of when we're thinking about getting back into like this kind of the business, like the coaching and all those kind of things. And I was like, well, I'm going to do it again. Like who don't want to serve? And I was like, well, I want to serve like real entrepreneurs, not like the ones that are like, I want to be rich on the internet. I'm like, I want real people who actually like are excited by this. And I was like, people like me where I can share the cool weird crap and they're going to be like, oh my gosh, that was awesome. As opposed to like, yeah, that's cool. But how do I buy a domain name? Like I don't kind of stuck on like that part, you know? And one good thing that we told Adam over and over and over again is like when you're figuring out your who, like one of the biggest things is make sure that like you're finding people to actually have money. Like rule number one is don't sell broke people, right? Like last year in Stokely we had him out there and we like tattooed it to his arm. So at the last inner circle meeting, the big problem was I was selling to broke people. And so Garrett White takes a Sharpie and he already sharpies it all over my arm. Man, the light's blinding as I asked and smack me in the face and then like appeared. All right. So that's the power that like it's huge. And after you figure out like who do you want to serve and it's like where are these people at? And that comes down to traffic. We'll get into that deeper throughout today and throughout the weekend. But the next thing is like what's the bait we're going to create? Like when I understood this, it was like if I create bait that attracts rich or poor people, I'm going to get poor people, right? Like I love poor people, but like I don't want to sell broke people. Like I don't want to like try convince them to give me the last $5,000. That's a horrible business to be in. But I put the right bait out there. I'll get people where it's like, oh, they want that. It's like, like all of you guys in here, no offense. You were the easiest sales in the world. I was like, you want to come as $25,000? You're like, yeah, we're in. Like it's so much easier, right? Like you bring the right people in like selling becomes way easier. It's just a whole different thing. But it comes down like what's the bait you're going to put out that gets the right people in? Okay. When you're selling like how to get rich on the internet stuff, you can get crappy people. You sell like, um, Oslo, you bury like how lose weight. You don't do anything. Just pop these magic pills. You lose weight. You're going to get those kind of people. You want the real people, the people we actually want to work with, you have to create something different. That's what gets the right person in. So for us, the first bait we created was a 108 split test book, which still sells like crazy by the way. It's so cool. We put it out there and we started selling those and it was a free plus shipping and we didn't make any money on it. But we were like, it was interesting. The people that bought it, like the people on my list at the time, no one bought it, but like you guys all started buying it. Like the right people started buying it. And it was a scary transition point because we were making a bunch of money over here. We talked about this last night a little bit like we're making money over here and then we're like, okay, we're going to stop this. We shut it down. We start this. And like sales were just like, oh, like crashing and burning where like we almost lost the entire company again. I almost burned this thing down like three times. So hopefully that's inspirational for you guys. And like it was this horrible thing. But then also like the right people started buying or start buying and like we started getting the right customers in, but we're like, we didn't know what else to sell these guys and we had to go through a lot of the process we'll talk about today to figure out like what the right people actually wanted. And we figured that out. Then it was just like, who it had completely transformed and took off and changed everything. So it's key is like figuring out who you actually want to serve and then the bait that will attract them and then making your business focus on the results. Like that's, that's, I'm going to talk about that for a day and we probably will deeper, but results, results are the key. All right. So I know a lot of you guys have, most of you guys have businesses and so, but I want to go through this process that I talked about in the book and it was, it was a fun experience when we were working through this section in the book and trying to, trying to really get deep into understanding it and explaining it. And I think it was valuable even if you have a business like just looking at like the market you're serving, like if there's tweaks or changes that'll, that'll exponentially open up things for you. So as I was going through this, I realized there's only really three core markets. Like every one of your businesses fall into one of these things. It's either wealth, health, or relationships. There's a fourth one, let me know, but I thought for a long time and if I actually don't want to know because the books are just on the publisher. So don't let me know. But for the most part they're all going to fit into one of these three, right? And so you think about this like, like at the beginning of the expert secret world, when people started becoming like experts in things, like they kind of were one of these three things, right? Was health, wealth, relationships and they were like, hey, I'm going to teach you how to lose weight. And all of a sudden everyone's like, oh, and they like made tons of money, right? Because they were the first dudes and everyone's like, oh, this is awesome. And it was really, really easy. But then everyone's like, well, that guy's making a lot of money. I can do that too. And they start jumping. And soon what happened is that each of these three markets became this. They became the Red Ocean, Blue Ocean concept, right? Because it's Red Ocean, now there's 10, 20, 30, 50, 100, 1000 people all in the same thing, all going for the same fish, the same thing. And so the water becomes super bloody, right? And so what happened is that markers, business, like people like us had figured out, okay, well, I need like everyone's fighting for the same customer. Like how can I, like how can I shift this to get away from the blood and get into a Blue Ocean? So you didn't read the book Red Ocean, Blue Ocean, it's a great book, but it's about this whole concept of like the Red Ocean of competition versus the Blue Ocean where you're unique and you're your own, right? And so what happened next is, I'm sure, I don't know the history lesson behind it, but I'm pretty sure this is when things started actually splintering out. So now you got wealth, you break it out like inside of wealth, there's finance, investing, real estate, sales, internet marketing. There's a million different, like we call them in the book, some submarkets, right? Same thing in relationships, you got love, dating, marriage, all things. Then in health, you got nutrition, straight training, weight loss, diet. There's like all these things started sprouting, all these little Blue Oceans, right? So everyone's popping these different Blue Oceans. And for a while, like there was one dude who was the sales guy, right? I look back, the guys I learned from Nish, like Kennedy and those guys are like Zig Zig, where was the sales guy? Dan Kennedy was the director response guy. Like they were just kind of in their own little Blue Oceans, and that's why they made all this money because they were the only ones. But then they got really good and trained, Dan Kennedy trained everybody and then everybody goes out and becomes hit little hymns and they're all fighting the same pond and suddenly this Blue Ocean, it was amazing, becomes all red and choppy and bloody and that's like, how do we compete here now, right? And so for us, that's the world that we've inherited, that's where you guys are at right now. And so a lot of you guys, a lot of people, they jump in and like, hey, I'm a diet guru, I'm a finance guru, and they jump into this thing and you're already in red waters. So you can go and you're fighting with everybody else, but you can't, it's hard to win when you're fighting with potentially some markets, thousands and thousands of people, right? And so for you, you gotta figure out, okay, we gotta go one step further, we're in this red market, if I picked whatever sub market, I've gotta go and I gotta figure out a new one and I've gotta create my own niche. Now emphasize the word create on purpose. Every person I learned from up to this point, we always tell them that, go pick a niche, go pick which niche you wanna be in. If you pick a niche, that means you're stepping into a red ocean. Like if it's something you can pick, it's already created, there's somebody there. So you can step into that, but you're not, maybe you have less competition, but you're still in a red ocean. So you have to feel like, how do I create my own niche within this? Where you can carve out your spot in this ecosystem. I had a friend that's in there, he's a brilliant guy, Michael Lovic, some of you guys may know him. He's got a couple different companies, but he was the first one to explain this to me, he said that when you go into a market, there's an ecosystem, right? And the ecosystem is a whole bunch of people, there's the guy doing this, the girl doing this, and there's all these different people. And it's like, you can go and compete, I'm gonna do what that guy's doing. But then like, since you're doing that, now there's this weird, now you're competing. And then all his friends won't like you because you're competing. And it clouds up this whole ecosystem, makes it like, again, bloody waters, right? Well, if you come in the ecosystem, like, hey, here's the health market. All right, there's a dude doing that, someone doing that. Like, here's all the people, and it's like, where do I fit? If you can find your spot that's different, then all the other people no longer like these competitors with you, suddenly they become your best partners and all these other things, like there's tons of benefit from that. But it's coming down to like, creating your own niche and figuring out like, what is your spot inside of this whole ecosystem? In the book, some examples I was giving, I kind of gave out, if you look at this. So, let's do this. So here's the market. From there, we'll move to the sub market. And then here, this is where you're creating your thing, your niche, right? And so, if you're from Canada, they call niche. If you're from America, we call niche, right? Is there any other variations of that? I think that's most of them. And so, for example, like, look at this, let's say I am in the wealth market. Let's say I'm wealth. And then over here, my sub market is, let's say it's real estate. I do real estate coaching or whatever. And then from here, I'm like, okay, well, there's like the flipping people, how to flip houses, there's how to short sell, there's how to, no money down, lease options, like what are all the different niches? So I can go and like jump into bloody water. I can be like, hey, what can I create that's different than everyone else? I had a friend, and this may be Red Waters now, but I had a friend like 10 years ago where he started doing the thing, he'd go to eBay, and he'd find these crappy houses for selling eBay that are like $100, because people were, like the government would get them back and then post them for like $100 by this house, horrible crappy houses. We found out this thing where he'd go to eBay and he'd like see a house for like $100, he'd buy it, and then he'd like get the listing, and then he'd repost it on eBay, and he'd rewrite the headline, some copy, and put a couple pictures on there, and he'd repost it, and he'd buy it for about $100, and he'd flip it for like $3,000 or $4,000, and he made millions of dollars doing that. And then he created a whole course on that, and so it's like, that is like, like eBay house flipping. Like that now is this new niche he created that nobody else is doing that, right? And if he goes back to all the like the house flipping guys and like all the other people in that niche, like they're gonna, like there's partnership and there's no competition, because he's the only one doing that, right? So for all of our businesses, we gotta figure that out. It's like, can I create my own unique spot, my own blue ocean, where I'm the only person in that thing? If you can do that, then like this game becomes so much easier. Did you look at some of that, what's your business like? Well, it's kind of like so-and-so, if you can say that, like that's probably not a good thing. I don't wanna say it's like, no, like we're gonna do something different. Like this is our spot. And sometimes when you're on stage talking, all of a sudden like something starts coming out and you're like, oh crap, like I shouldn't tell a story, right? And then it's too late because people are like, well, you have to keep going with it. And all these people are like, I have twins. I don't want to tell me their twin story and then they signed up. Like how do we, like what things should we share? And obviously I don't share everything with my life. Like I'm very selective in what I do and I don't share, but I put things out specifically for reasons to get different people to come in and to be part of it. And so that's kind of this whole track to characters about it's like figuring that out. And we're going a lot deeper in this over the next three days, but just a couple of quick things. Number one, every good attractive character has to have a good backstory. I think it's tomorrow we're going to go really deep into story, which I'm so excited. I've never taught this stuff and I teach tomorrow. I'm just like crazy excited. So we're going to go deep into backstory. But the biggest thing to understand is that each of you guys as an expert, like the rest of the world looks at you and like you're on this hill and they're like, wow, like look what they did. Like they're amazing. So you, for you to be relatable, then to want to be, to become part of your world, you have to get off of your mountain like hike back down, where they're at, come back to where they're starting and be like, Hey man, I've been over there is awesome. But I was here too. Let me talk to you about that. And we tell the story here and we bring them with us. And that's what gets people to bond with you and have empathy with you and all the good stories telling stuff. We'll talk about tomorrow starts from that backstory. If you're getting off your pedestal, coming back down to them and having empathy and relating to them. If you do that, then they will follow you anywhere you want to go. Number two is attractive character speaking parables, speaking stories, which is what all tomorrow is going to be about. We're going to get really good. You guys are going to have a chance to tell your stories and building out inventories of stories, a bunch of stuff tomorrow we're going to be doing, but it's getting really good telling story after story after story. We've been live for like an hour and a half so far. I don't know if I'm just keeping track. How many stories I think I've told so far in the last hour and a half? The 80s. Is that the real number? I don't know yet, but the more you get comfortable with that, that's what gets people engaged and gets them not falling asleep at their desk. Third thing is character flaws. You have to be, this is so hard. You have to open those things up and share your flaws. I'm going to share a personal story about Natalie. She doesn't know yet. I don't know Natalie super well. I met her yesterday as the first time person, but I've been stalking her and watching her and understanding her in her business a little bit. What's interesting is she super cares about great inventories, all these kind of things, but I was interested in what caused people to follow her. I went to one of your pages and you have your story. Do you want to talk? You want to tell that story? I saw that and I was like, that's why people are in love with her. It was so good and so powerful and it was sharing a character flaw. I am involved in the health and fitness industry and I kind of fell into it by accident, but where my social media falling just exploded was when I talked about this concept of perfectly imperfect. After I had two kids and they were 10 pound babies, my stomach was way, way, way out here. I gained 70 pounds and after I had them, after I had my daughter, I remember looking in the mirror and being like, my body is so different than I used to be. In my head I thought I was an athlete, but I remember looking in the mirror and being like, why doesn't anybody talk about this? Nobody told me that I was going to come home and still look six months pregnant. Nobody told me. I was like kind of mad actually. So I flipped my camera out and I bent over and I showed that I have really like a lot. I'm in really good shape, but I have a lot of stretch skin on my stomach and I posted that and I was like so scared. I was like to click the button. My hand was like, oh my gosh, this is awful. Everybody's going to be like, oh my gosh, that's disgusting. And it was amazing. What happened is that people just, it was like a magnet. People just flocked to it. And so what I did is I used that idea and I reached out to some other big people like Heidi Powell. People have big followings and I created like one video that went totally viral where I shared all these things. Like you might see me now with my hair perfectly done in makeup, but this is what I really look like, you know, with bags under my eyes. And I showed like, you might see this beautiful home, but what you don't see is, and I showed through my house like every single picture frame has a stock photo in it because I suck at decorating. Like I am not a decorator. I showed my skin. I said, you might see these professional photographs and see that, you know, this beautiful six pack abs. But when I bend over and I showed like the really, really wrinkly skin, you know, I just went through like a whole list of things. And so people connected to that. All of a sudden people just said, oh my gosh, I feel that way too. And I'm not alone. And somebody gets me. And once like I connected on that level, it was like, I could, anything I put out, people would buy because all of a sudden everybody said, oh my gosh, I'm not alone. The vulnerability, like the level of shame that surrounded some of those sensitive topics went way down. And people all of a sudden wanted to be a part of that. And so every single product that I've created since has been around authenticity, vulnerability, talking about things that nobody else will talk about. Like I just released an e-book. Like after you have a baby, I don't know anybody here is moms or is a man who has kids. But a lot of times there's pelvic floor damage that happens after you have a baby. And when women jump on the trampoline, they pee their pants. And nobody talks about it. Like every woman I know struggles with it. Nobody talks about it. But I just, like you said, I partnered, I'm not an expert in that area, but I partnered with a doctor who helped me co-write the program. We launched it on Monday and it's been wildly successful because again, it's something that nobody talks about. So going back to what you're saying, sorry that took your time. That was really long. But what I've kept into is that when you're willing to like be really authentic, you can't, if you're too calculated about it, it doesn't work. You actually have to be authentic behind it and you're willing to be vulnerable and share things that like make your stomach turn a little bit. Like I, on this launch, I literally peed my pants doing a workout on camera. I have a company called Della Recal Club where we provide daily at home videos. Before I did this program, literally the film was rolling the whole time and you can see on my gray shorts, the spot getting bigger and bigger. And I was like mortified to share those pictures. And guess what? I shared it and got like, it was just insane. People were like, oh my gosh, me too. So once I was able to tap into that, like being willing to put yourself out there on things that feel, here's the thing about vulnerability is that it feels, it makes you feel small and it feels scary, but to everybody else it looks brave and it looks courageous. And so, yeah. That was awesome. Thank you. Thank you for sharing. Yeah. Thank you for sharing that. It's powerful. You think about superheroes, Superman. It's the most boring character on Earth. They introduced Kryptonite. Like, for some reason we all think we have to be Superman, but like nobody cares until there's like the flaw. When you're willing to share those things, like that's, things open up. Twice in the two years since Click Phones launched, you guys remember the, we've gone down and I'm like, duh. And lead pages went down. If you guys remember the lead pages went down, crickets. They were down for like a day and a half. Nobody said anything. Like, the company was quiet. When we went down, I get on Facebook Live, pissed off as it can be with my scruffy face and like in my bedroom or where I was in the UK one time and just, and I'd share like how pissed I am. Like this isn't okay. And like, man, like I was expecting losing thousands of members during our outage and instead, nothing, like not a single thing. People are rallying behind us. I got companies calling, people calling us like, man, we're here for it. And like, it's different. Like when you share those things, it's crazy. Whereas if you hide it, man, just like, it's interesting. So, and then number four here is polarity and we're getting to this as well, story. But like, not always trying to be perfect and like, oh, I'm going to share the cool thing. Like the more you're willing to be polar, the better. And we'll kind of go into that. But there's, and there's different places and different times to use it. But it's a big key as well. All right. And then I don't have too much time to go into this, but typically as an expert, you're going to pick one of these identities, either a leader, adventure, crusader, reporter, evangelist, reluctant hero. Again, we don't have time to go too deep into that, but it's kind of like, how are you being presented? Like, how are you talking to your audience? Some people have made huge livings off of being the reporter and interviewing people and those kind of things. Other people, it's standing up and being the leader. Other people, it's, you know, more like the reluctant hero and like, just kind of figuring out which personality type you want to interact with your audience and really understanding that as well. All right. So a couple rules I want to go through attractive character. I'm learning how this whole thing works. Okay. First step is you have to live the life that your audience wishes they could live. Okay. People want to become like you. They want to do cool stuff and most of us are all private. That's why I do Snapchat. Like I share my life. Sometimes it's awkward. My wife gets pissed every single time. She hates it, right? But I share those things because people see stuff. They see me in my cryosonic freezing. They see me in the float tank. They see me in the wrestling room lifting weights with my kids. They see me, like, I'm showing those things because I want people getting a little glimpse of that. And I'm like, go. I don't want to cryosonic. It's kind of weird, but like, I want to be able to do stuff that I want to do. That's weird. That's what I want to do. I want to give people like hope that like this is, this is what's possible if you follow the stuff I'm doing. Like these are the things I'm doing. And you're maybe different, but this is what is important to me. And because of what this is happening, I get to do those things. And so it's important. Like that's, it's, you know, I think there's like the cheese side of it where a lot of people are out there and they're driving their Lambos and like that kind of stuff. It's not what I'm talking about. Like that, that'll attract the wrong people into your world. But it's like the, it's getting shown kind of behind the curtains of what life is really like. That's what gets the right people in. And like, man, like that's, that's cool. I'm going to be able to hang out with my daughter and drink wake shakes in the morning. I want to be able to, you know, do those kind of things and like having those things out there. But it comes back to like, like living a cool life, like doing cool stuff and letting people see that. Because that's, that's a big, a big part of it. Second thing, as a leader, you have to maintain absolute certainty. Satema was in the last inner circle group. He had a whole presentation on, on self-confidence. He said, self-confidence is for children. Like certainty is for men. Okay. If you look at any situation, whoever comes into a situation, the person who's most certain will always win. Okay. And if you're going to be the expert and be leading people, you can't be like, oh, wish you wash, you know, what do you think? Like you have to be certain in that thing. Okay. And so like having absolute certainty. Any leader has to have this. If you're kind of like, I got self-confidence. Yeah. I think we can win. Like if you think you're going to win, you're not going to win. You have to be like, I know I'm going to win. Dan Gable, who's the greatest wrestler, wrestler and probably the greatest wrestling coach of all time. Those who follow wrestling, he, he said he will not let one of his wrestlers go out on the mat unless he knows he's going to win. So if you come like, I think I'm going to win coach. He pulls you off the roster. You're not allowed to go. Like he only let people who knew they were going to win step out on the mat. It's a big deal. Like I was in an event where a guy was on stage and he was trying to pitch a mastermind group and he's like, well, I'm going to do a mastermind. I'm thinking like, I think like five grand or 10 grand. Like what do you guys like, how many, we have five grand, 10 grand. Okay. And then maybe I'm like, dude, like I do not want to give you anything. If you came here, this is how it's going to work. Can you watch Garrett White step on stage and like this is how it's going to work guys. You're going to pay me this much money and this is how you're going to do it. And this is when you're going to do it. You're like, all right, here you go. Right. Like absolute certainty wins in every situation. This is true in business, true in relationship, everything like absolute certainty wins. Okay. Number three here is do not be boring. Oh, that's like, just don't be boring. Like, okay. This is my little design I designed to show this. This is my prolific index. Okay. This is how you know if you're boring and there's like the boring side and then there's like the crazy side. Okay. So like right here in the middle is the mainstream on the sides are crazy and in the middle is prolific. This is where you make a bunch of money. Okay. So if you're mainstream, I'm going to the weight loss market for example, because that's the easiest one to illustrate this right. So in the middle, you're like the government, they used to be the, I guess it used to be the food pyramid or the four food groups. I found out my publisher, that's not a real thing anymore. That's like the food plate. It's my kids got these plates from my mom and it's like, it's like so jacked up. Like the carbs are like half of it and then like all these things and like there's your plate, they just fill it up and it's horrible. Right. That's the mainstream. That's teaching in school. It's like so boring. If you can learn it in school, it's not going to make you any money. Okay. That's why school's got to trick you and a whole bunch of other ways to get your money because they teach you crap. It's all in the mainstream. Then on the outsides of it are like the crazy zone. Okay. So if you go out here, this is like, how do you guys see the movie, eat the sun? This movie is actually really awesome. Am I the only one that's seen it? All right. So somewhere between like the mainstream and the crazy is that it's a little spot in the middle that I call the prolific zone where like people make money. Okay. So the weight loss space, you know, Dave Astor's with my friends. He, he had this idea that was kind of like a little crazy, but not quite too crazy. He's like, Hey, I went on this and he had a story. We'll talk about the story time. Like I was on this hike in the Himalayan mountains and I got lost and I was cold and I was hungry. I met these people and they took me inside. They had yak butter and they put inside this coffee and I drank it and that energy in my brain and I couldn't figure it out. So I went back home and I realized that butter is good for you. And I started putting butter in my coffee. Now this is a huge thing. And he called it bull proof. And now he's got this huge mass movement of people who put butter in their coffee. Okay. It's not, it's kind of crazy, but not quite like insane. And it's not really mainstream. It's in the middle here. That gets traction. That gets media. That gets people listening. That gets people talking. Um, and you look at Dave Ashby, like he's one of the worst salespeople I've ever seen. That his, his, uh, everything he sells is just like, Hey guys, this is some weird thing I take. And he explains it in some super nerdy way. It has an order link and people buy it because that whole butter story, they're bought into that. Like that was his thing. And so for all you guys, it's like figuring out that piece where you're, you're not in the mainstream and you're not crazy. Cause if you understand that, like that's, that's the sweet spot where people give you a bunch of money. And the second thing is, is in the, in here, this is like the polarity as well. Um, if you look at something and you have this thing, right? And if you start becoming polar where there's like this, this polarization that happens, like in the polar zones is where, where people are, or we start making money. Okay. If you're neutral, like nobody cares. Okay. A lot of times we try like, we don't want to offend people. Like this whole political thing has been really hard. Here's the one sentence. You guys ready? It says this, people will do anything for those who encourage their dreams, justify their failures, lie their fears, confirm their suspicions and help them throw rocks at their enemies. And when I'm teaching and talking and I'm doing things, like I'm trying to weave this in all the time. For you, it's belief in yourself. For them, it's belief in like this thing that you're trying to create. So how do we create belief? Story for me. So I'm calling this one, How to Break the Four Minute Mile. So you guys know the story about Four Minute Mile? Roger Bannister in 1954 was the first dude who ever broke the Four Minute Mile. And after that, a whole bunch of people have done it. Like, but nobody thought it was possible. Like human beings cannot break it. And as soon as someone did it, it was like, oh, I believe it's possible. And then they can go and they can actually do it. Sydney happened to be so August 17th, 2004. So that was, and what, 12, 13 years ago-ish? I was just getting into this whole business. My goal when I got started was if I can make $1,000 a month, I'll be rich. Like that's all. If I had a thousand bucks a month, that would do everything. Because I think my wife and I just got married. Our mortgage was $4.75 a month for our little duplex for renting. We had a little car payment. So like a thousand bucks to cover all of our hard costs. And then like, whatever my wife made, she's making $9.50 an hour. Like, that's like we go blow it on dates and like going bowling and stuff. That'd be awesome. So that's why I got in this whole business was like a thousand bucks a month. That was my whole goal. So I'm doing the whole thing. I'm learning. I'm having fun. I'm selling stuff on eBay and Craigslist and doing all sorts of stuff. Have a good time. And then about that time, there was this guy that was coming out. And everyone started kind of whispering and talking about this guy named John Reese. And I don't know what it was, I kind of researched in my case this guy. That's about all I knew. And then he started doing this pre-launch for this course called Traffic Secrets. He's like, we're doing this course. We're going to launch it and all this stuff. And he has kind of fun to watch and see the model of what he's doing. So he went out there and he goes and he does this big launch. And when the launch happened, I actually was going on vacation with my family down to a place called Bear Lake, which is like five hours south of here. This couple of guys have been at Bear Lake sounds like, which is like the coolest little town. And back then 13 years ago, there was no internet in the city at all. And so we got there and there's no internet. And so me, I had in my little brand new business thing, I had like three customers and I'm like stressing like, what if one of them emails me and they're going to be pissed and like refund the $17 I got? Like I was all panicking, right? So I'm sitting there on Bear Lake for like three or four days, just all nervous and stuff. And finally I'm like, I got to get internet. And they were asking me, but finally someone's like, well, there's a library, a little tiny library. And if you go there, they've got dialy modems and you can actually jump on. I'm like, Colette, I have to go. Like, you know, kiss my customers email me. I can't like, I need to go. So go to the library. I go in there and I come in and there's like super quiet, you know, libraries are quite nush us and, and there's two computers and there's like two chairs and there's a line of like three or four people behind each one. I'm like, oh, I'm standing in the line. I'm like waiting. And it's like, I think it's like a 10 minute limit. And then the person gets off, the next person gets in and finally get closer. Finally, I'm just my time. So I jump on the computer and I start looking through my emails. I'm going through my email address. The time was moneyforcollegeatjuno.com. So I go to Juno, I log in and I see my emails in there. I'm looking, I'm looking through my emails and like, no, none of my customers emailed me and like, dang it. And then also I see one email from John Reese and it says like, sometimes I have something like, we did it or something like that. I was like, did what? So I click on the link to, to read it and I opened it up and just the day prior, uh, they had started their launch and he kind of told the story. He said, look, we did this launch. Uh, it was selling a thousand dollar course and the first 18 hours we sold a thousand copies. We made a million dollars in 18 hours. I remember like sitting there and like it was kind of weird situation. I was sitting there like everything behind me, they just disappeared. And I sat back in my chair and I was like a million dollars in a day, 18 hours. I was doing the math and I'm like, like a thousand dollar course. He just, he just sold a thousand. I was like, what if I create something, it's a thousand bucks. I sold like three a day. I gotta be a millionaire within a year. I was like, oh my gosh. And all of a sudden like the, the wills in my head starts spinning and like all of a sudden I believed it was possible. I was it, I had belief like this is actually, I could actually do this and my thousand dollar month goal is stupid because like I could actually make a million dollars. I never thought it, I never even like crossed my mind, never believed it. And as soon as John had done it, I believed I could do it. And it's interesting is that after that, within a year, I almost made a million dollars. I missed, I'll try to do like an account a year and I've missed it by like, I don't know, like a hundred grand or something. So the next year I tried again, I missed it by like whatever, by third year I hit a million dollars in a 12 month period of time. And I look back and I was like, man, if it wasn't the fact that John did, I wouldn't even try it because he did, I had belief that I could do it. And so I started thinking about that as we're building our, our culture. I was like, how do we, how do we cause belief for our people? Because if they all believe that inside of phones, they can like have all their wildest dreams come true. Like if they believe that, like how would that change things? And so that time we went through, I was wondering, I was like, I wonder how many people inside of ClickFones have actually made over a million dollars. So we went through the database and we export and we can't see all sales. We can see Stripe sales and a couple other things. We export it all. And so the first initial export, I think there was 51 people that made a million dollars. I was like, are you kidding me? We made 51 millionaires so far. And that's the only people we can track. So I started putting fillers out there. And in the last few months we found out like we're just shy of a hundred people that made a million dollars in ClickFunnels. That at least one funnel. Multiple people have like three, four, five, like multiple funnels that made a million dollars. And so as we're coming towards this event, I was like, man, we need to do something to like, because everyone realized that all their peers are doing it. Like it becomes so real. Like they have so much belief in it. So we put together a trophy. I don't know if you guys have seen this yet. This is the Two Comma Club. So the Two Commas is Two Commas a million dollars. For those of you guys who aren't Mormon in the audience, I'm going to tell you guys a Mormon story, but it's history lessons as well. So in the Book of Mormon, there's a story about a guy named Captain Moroni. And Captain Moroni was running a huge army of people and he was going into a war that he could not win. And it was so scary. Things were happening and people, he was leading started freaking out and like, we're not going to win this war. So they started leaving and going to the other guys and started fighting in that other direction. He's like, my army's leaving me and then fighting against a kind of panic. Like, what do I do? What do I do? So what he did is he went and he took his coat off. I can imagine like, I wish this was a movie. It's so cool to see. He rips his coat and on the side of the coat, he writes on it. He said, memory of our God, our religion and freedom and our peace, our wives and our children. And he fastened it to a pole and he put it out there. Boom. And he said, this is the title of liberty. So this is what we're fighting for. You guys. And if you posted that, all these people said, that's what we're fighting for. And they came back and they went to this war. They shouldn't have won. And they came in and they beat everyone. And I always look at that. And I think about like in Braveheart, Ramel Gibson has the same speech, right? He's in front of you. He's on the horse riding through you and yelling out freedom and like everyone like, there's this thing, this title of liberty, this thing where it's like, look, we're going to be free from whatever it is. Because I'm kind of behind the scenes on, or I'm kind of slower moving through on these ones. But if you look at ours, I'll read it. You can kind of just hear how we, how we positioned our. It says, FunnelHackers, a new breed of entrepreneur, smarter, leaner, faster and free. FunnelHackers wage guerrilla war against the big brands and they win. FunnelHackers start without a safety net, venture capital is a four letter word and bootstrap is their middle name. FunnelHackers never go public because their products serve the public, not a board of directors. This is like a whole us versus them that we're trying to bring out. FunnelHackers never ask for permission and they are never shackled to investors. FunnelHacker creates value and puts ethics before conversions. If you ask the MBAs or look at their college textbooks, they'll tell you that what we're doing is impossible. It's happening every single day. FunnelHackers define their own destiny, they create their own luck, they build their own empires, they change the world. FunnelHackers are motivated knowing they are just one funnel away. I am a funnel hacker. So that's, that's one of ours. My name is Russell Brunson and I'm part of an underground group of entrepreneurs that you've probably never even heard of before. We don't rely on cash from venture capitalists to get started and we don't even have goals to go public either. In fact, our motivation is the exact opposite. You see, we have products and services and things that we know change people's lives. We don't have financial safety nets because every test we take is with our own money. We have to be profitable from day number one. So how do we do that? How is that even possible?
